transcript: Hey everyone, today's episode is all about how fast the world of technology is changing. American mathematician Ernest Ryu from the University of California, Los Angeles, shared that together with GPT5 Pro, he managed to prove the convergence of Nester Robov's accelerated method, a unsolved for more than 40 years. This method proposed in 1983 became one of the key tools in optimization theory. But until now, mathematicians hadn't been able to rigorously prove that the algorithm always converges to a solution. According to Ryu, GPT5 Pro acted as his research partner. The model generated possible mathematical constructions, lemmas, [music] and formulas while he selected the viable ones and turned them into formal proofs. The main idea was to find what's known as decreasing energy functions, special indicators that continuously diminish, showing that the algorithm is moving closer to the minimum point. With their help, Ryu proved two major things. First, that in the continuous version of the algorithm, the trajectory doesn't drift among multiple potential solutions, but instead stabilizes at a single point. Second, that the discrete version, the one used in real world machine learning optimization, also converges to a single minimum under standard mathematical conditions, assuming the function is convex, smooth, and has a finite minimum. It removed the barriers that had traditionally kept such powerful technology confined to corporate research and development departments. Students started using it to help with homework. Professionals began streamlining their workflows and creatives found a new muse for their projects. The public awakening was swift and all-encompassing, setting the stage for an unprecedented pace of innovation that would follow in the coming years. What began as an experiment quickly became a global utility. The story of Chat GPT is not just a story about algorithms and processing power. It is a story about us. It is about how we adapt, how we create, and how we learn when presented with a tool that amplifies our own intelligence. In just a few short years, this technology has evolved at a breathtaking speed. with each new version unlocking possibilities that were unthinkable just months before. From its initial debut to the sophisticated models we see today, the journey has been one of constant improvement, pushing the boundaries of what we believed was possible and fundamentally altering our relationship with technology itself. The initial fascination with chat GBT was rooted in its novelty. It was a remarkable curiosity, a digital oracle that could generate surprisingly humanlike text on almost any topic. People asked it to write jokes, compose song lyrics in the style of famous artists, or create fantastical stories. These early interactions were playful, driven by a sense of wonder and a desire to test the limits. Yet, as the novelty began to wear off, something more significant started to take its place. Users quickly discovered that beyond the entertaining tricks, Chat GPT possessed a profound utility. The tool was not just a toy. It was a powerful assistant that could solve realworld problems. This transition from a source of amusement to an essential professional instrument happened remarkably quickly. It was not a model designed to dazzle with entirely new features like multimodality or deep reasoning. Instead, its purpose was far more practical and for many professional users far more important. It was built for stability. GPT 4.5 represented a concerted effort to sand down the rough edges of its predecessors, creating an AI that was more consistent, more predictable, and fundamentally more trustworthy for day-to-day use. The primary focus of this update was on tackling the persistent problem of AI hallucinations. While earlier models had made progress, they could still occasionally generate plausible but entirely false information. GPG 4.5 was engineered to dramatically reduce these instances through more advanced training techniques and internal factchecking mechanisms. The model became significantly more grounded in reality. It was better at citing its sources, more cautious when speculating, and more likely to state that it did not know an answer rather than invent one. This pursuit of factual accuracy was a clear signal that open AAI was prioritizing the needs of users who relied on chat GPT for critical facts sensitive work. Um, another key area of improvement was in its creative consistency. While models like GPT4 were highly creative, their output could sometimes be erratic, requiring significant human editing to maintain a consistent tone or style over a long piece of writing. GPT 4.5 addressed this headon. It was fine-tuned to better understand and adhere to stylistic instructions, making it a more reliable partner for creative professionals. A writer could ask it to draft multiple chapters of a story in a specific author's style and the model would maintain that voice with far greater fidelity. This made it less of a wild brainstormer and more of a disciplined creative assistant. This shift in focus from revolution to refinement was a sign of a maturing technology. The initial wow factor of AI was being supplemented by a growing demand for dependability. Users did not just want an AI that was powerful. They wanted an AI they could count on. GPT4.5 was the answer to that call. It was less about adding new tricks and more about perfecting the existing ones. It was a quieter, less flashy update, but its emphasis on polish and stability made it one of the most significant steps towards making AI a truly indispensable tool for professionals who valued precision above all else. The refinements introduced with GPT 4.5 quickly established it as the trusted workhorse version of chat GPT. This was the model that businesses, researchers, and serious creators turned to when the stakes were high and accuracy was non-negotiable. Its stability meant that professionals could integrate it more deeply into their workflows with a higher degree of confidence. A legal firm using the AI to summarize case law could be more certain that the output would be factually sound. A financial analyst using it to interpret reports could trust its summaries to be free from inventive hallucinated data, making it a reliable foundation for their own analysis. One of the most appreciated improvements was its ability to handle long- form writing without drifting off topic. Earlier models when tasked with generating a lengthy document like a report or a detailed proposal would often start strong but gradually lose focus, introducing irrelevant information or forgetting the initial instructions. GPT4.5 demonstrated a much stronger ability to maintain coherence and thematic consistency over thousands of words. This made it exceptionally useful for producing structured professional documents. It could draft an entire business plan or a technical manual while staying tightly aligned with the initial outline, saving users countless hours of editing and rewriting. This enhanced reliability, also extended to its coding capabilities. While previous models were excellent at generating code snippets, GPT 4.5 was better at understanding the context of an entire project. It produced code that was not only functionally correct but also cleaner, more efficient, and better commented. It followed established coding conventions more rigorously and was less likely to introduce subtle bugs. For software development teams, this made it a more dependable partner in the coding process. It was the difference between an assistant who gives you clever but sometimes messy ideas and a senior colleague who provides robust production ready solutions. Ultimately GPT 4.5 solidified chat GPT's role as an essential tool in the professional world. Its value proposition was no longer just about speed or creativity but about trust. It became the go-to model for any task where the cost of error was high. This focus on dependable, highquality performance may have been less exciting to the general public, but for the millions of people using AI as a core part of their job, it was the most important upgrade yet. It transformed chat GPT from a powerful but occasionally unpredictable collaborator into a steadfast and reliable workhorse that could be counted on to deliver. Just a few months after the release of the stability focus GPT4.5, OpenAI raised the bar once again in May 2025 with GPT4.1. This update was not about stability in isolation. It was about combining the high reasoning ability of its predecessors with even greater speed and an exceptional talent for following highly specific complex instructions. GPT 4.1 was designed for the power user who needed both intelligence and precision. It represented a synthesis of the best attributes of previous models, creating a tool that was not only smart and reliable, but also remarkably adept at understanding and executing user intent with surgical accuracy. One of the standout features of GPT 4.1 was its improved instruction following capability, often referred to as steerability. Users could provide it with a long list of intricate constraints, negative prompts, and formatting requirements, and the model would adhere to them with impressive fidelity. For example, you could ask it to write a product description that was exactly 150 words long, used a cheerful but not informal tone, mentioned three specific features, and avoided a list of five particular words. GPT4.1 could handle this complex web of instructions far more reliably than any version before it, giving users an unprecedented level of control over the final output. This model also brought significant enhancements to its coding skills, building upon the foundations laid by earlier versions. It demonstrated a deeper understanding of niche programming languages and complex software frameworks. More importantly, it excelled at tasks that required both coding and reasoning, such as refactoring legacy code to meet modern standards or writing comprehensive unit tests for an existing application. This combination of highle logic and practical coding implementation made it an invaluable asset for software engineers, helping them to not only write code faster, but also to improve its overall quality, robustness, and maintainability. GPT 4.1 was a masterclass in balance. It did not sacrifice intelligence for speed, nor did it trade precision for broad capability. It managed to deliver improvements across the board, making it arguably the most versatile and powerful generalpurpose model to date. For professionals who needed to generate highly specific content, whether it was legal clauses, technical documentation, or precisely formatted marketing copy, GPT 4.1 was a gamecher. It represented a new peak in the evolution of AI where the machine was not just a knowledgeable partner but also an obedient and highly skilled executive of complex tasks. Alongside the flagship GPT 4.1 model, OpenAI released a companion that was just as significant GPT4.1 Mini. This version was built on a different philosophy, prioritizing speed and cost efficiency above all else. While it did not possess the sheer intellectual horsepower of its larger sibling, the Mini model was still remarkably capable, far more so than earlier generations like GPT 3.5, but it could deliver its responses at a fraction of the cost and with significantly lower latency. This was a strategic move designed to make high quality AI accessible for a completely new range of applications where speed was the most critical factor. The primary target for GPT 4.1 Mini was the world of rapid high volume tasks and mobile use. Think of customer service chat bots that need to handle thousands of conversations simultaneously or AI powered features integrated directly into smartphone apps. For these use cases, a response that takes several seconds is simply too slow. The mini model was optimized to provide near instantaneous replies, making it perfect for powering real-time conversational interfaces, content moderation systems, and quick data extraction tasks. It brought the power of advanced AI to scenarios where cost and response time had previously been prohibitive barriers. This focus on efficiency did not mean a complete sacrifice of quality. GPT 4.1 Mini was carefully trained to be highly effective at a range of common tasks such as summarization, classification, and standard content generation. While it might not write a prize-winning novel or solve an Olympiad level maths problem, it was more than capable of drafting a professional email, summarizing a news article, or answering factual questions with high accuracy. This made it an ideal choice for embedding intelligence into everyday applications, providing a good enough AI experience that was fast, affordable, and reliable for the vast majority of user queries. The introduction of the mini model was a crucial step in the commoditization of powerful AI. It recognized that not every task requires a top-of-the-line resource inensive model. By providing a tiered offering, OpenAI allowed developers to choose the right tool for the job, optimizing for either peak performance or maximum efficiency. The introduction of agents was the moment Chat GPT truly began to fulfill the longheld promise of a personal digital assistant. It was no longer just a source of answers, but a source of action. Ultimately, the rise of powerful AI challenges us to double down on what makes us uniquely human. The AI era does not diminish the value of human intelligence. It reframes it. Our greatest task is not just to build smarter machines, but to become wiser, more creative, and more compassionate humans in the process, ensuring that technology serves our highest potential. We have traversed a remarkable journey from the first flicker of public consciousness with GPT 3.5 to the multimodal personalitydriven enterprise integrated powerhouse that is GPT5. We have seen AI learn to chat, to reason, to see, to hear, and to act. Each upgrade, each breakthrough has not only changed the tool itself, but has also subtly reshaped our world, altering how we work, learn, and create. The evolution has been breathtaking. a compressed timeline of innovation that has set a new pace for technological progress and left an indelible mark on our society in just a few short years. The story, however, does not end here. This deep dive has chronicled the past, but the future of chat GPT and artificial intelligence remains a vast unwritten book. The questions we posed about limitless context, proactive assistance, and the pursuits of AGI are not idle speculation. They are the likely themes of the very next chapters. The one thing we can be certain of is that the rate of change will continue to be relentless. The capabilities that seem like science fiction today will likely be standard features in the models of tomorrow, and the next chapter may be even more transformative than anything we have seen so far. This ongoing evolution places a profound responsibility on all of us as creators, users, and citizens. We are co-authoring this next chapter every single day through our interactions, our demands, and our ethical considerations. The future of AI is not a predetermined path. It is a landscape of possibilities that we have the power to shape. Staying informed, engaged, and thoughtful is no longer an option for tech enthusiasts alone. It is a necessity for everyone who wishes to navigate the world that is taking shape around us, ensuring that it develops in a way that benefits all of humanity. One thing is for sure, the story of Chat GPT is not over. It is a living, evolving narrative that continues to unfold with each new line of code, each new data set, and each new idea. What we have witnessed so far is merely the beginning. The opening act in a much larger drama. The next leap forward could come tomorrow or next year, but it will come. title: OpenAI’s Chaotic Week: GPT-5 Backlash, GPT-6 Scandal, and Google’s Big Comeback
transcript: This week, OpenAI found itself at the center of global attention for all the wrong reasons. The long-awaited GPT5 launch disappointed many. GPT6 sparked outrage over adult content, and a scientific misstep left experts laughing. Meanwhile, Google quietly made a powerful comeback. [music] It's a week that may define the future of AI and the credibility of the companies shaping it. Let's break down everything that happened. When OpenAI launched GPT5 in August, expectations were skyhigh. People imagined a massive leap forward, something that would redefine what artificial intelligence could do. Instead, the launch left many underwhelmed. The live demo was filled with awkward pauses, incorrect data charts, and performance hiccups that made even loyal fans uneasy. What was supposed to be a celebration of progress turned into a public relations problem. Sam Alman, OpenAI CEO, later admitted that the vibes at launch were bad. But he claimed things improved once people started using GPT5 in real world settings. Inside OpenAI, employees described the model as a true research partner, a system that doesn't just summarize information, but actively help scientists and engineers move their work forward. That's a bold claim, but the public wasn't convinced. For most users, GPT5 didn't feel dramatically smarter than GPT4. It was faster, cheaper, and smoother. But the difference wasn't revolutionary. Many critics, including AI researcher Gary Marcus, argued that OpenAI had overpromised. He said the grand ideas of artificial general intelligence and PHD level reasoning still hadn't arrived. To him, GPT5 felt like an upgrade, not a breakthrough. Greg Brockman, OpenAI's co-founder, defended the release. He explained that the improvements weren't about using more data or bigger chips, but rather about how the model learned from humans. Reinforcement learning from human feedback, he said, played a much larger role this time. And Mark Chen, another OpenAI scientist, offered a perspective that most people missed. He revealed that in math heavy environments, GPT5's reasoning had jumped from average to world class, ranking among the top five in simulated math competitions. This detail impressed scientists, but didn't matter much to the average user writing emails or editing documents. To most people, the model just didn't feel next generation. Still, Altman stood firm. He promised that GPT6 would [music] be significantly better than 5 and GPT7 would go even further. That kind of confidence kept the hype alive. But then came the real controversy when OpenAI announced its plan to relax content restrictions in GPT6. Things got messy fast. The company revealed that verified adults would soon be allowed to access and generate sexually explicit text under certain safeguards. Supporters called it a step toward user freedom, treating adults like adults while keeping minors safe. But critics saw it as a moral shift, accusing OpenAI of chasing engagement and profit at the cost of ethics. Advocacy [music] groups warned that this decision could lead to serious problems from emotional dependency on AI chatbots to privacy risks. They pointed out that even with strict rules, systems like these are easy to exploit. Every country also defines adult content differently, which means one global policy could easily offend cultural or legal norms elsewhere. Inside OpenAI, the goal was framed differently. The company claimed this was about personalization and choice, letting people decide how human they want their AI to sound. But many experts weren't convinced that such fine-tuning could truly control the emotional and psychological effects of synthetic intimacy. As the debate over GPT6's new content rules grew louder, OpenAI found itself walking a very thin line between innovation and responsibility. The company said it wanted to give users more freedom. But critics argued it was opening the door to potential harm. Privacy advocates warned that if ChatGpt became a platform for adults to share intimate conversations, Open AAI would end up holding some of the most sensitive data imaginable. That kind of information would require extreme protection, advanced encryption, strict internal controls, and rapid response systems for any security breach. Ethics researchers raised a deeper concern. They explained that AI systems trained to always sound polite and agreeable often end up overaggreeing with users. In normal conversations, that might seem harmless, but in emotionally or sexually charged contexts, it could reinforce unhealthy behavior or even dangerous beliefs. On the other hand, some women and vulnerable users argued that a controlled textonly environment could feel safer than interacting [music] with strangers online. This revealed a complex truth. Safety and freedom can't always exist [music] in perfect balance. Europe's AI regulators quickly joined the conversation. They warned that even the best safety systems can be bypassed through jailbreaking, where users find creative ways to make AI break its own rules. For a company already under scrutiny, that meant higher moderation costs and faster response times. OpenAI conducted an internal review using over 500 prompts across a wide range of sensitive topics. The goal was not to make chat GPT pick sides, but to measure how easily the model's tone or framing could drift depending on the user's input. The results were surprising. The team found that under strong liberal leaning prompts, the model tended to shift tone and opinion more than it did with equally strong conservative ones. That imbalance created a perception problem. A manager at OpenAI posted that GPT5 had solved several of Paul Erdos's famous mathematical problems. Experts like Thomas Bloom and Terrence Tao called it a misunderstanding, not a miracle. They clarified that GPT5 had simply [music] found existing proofs buried deep in academic literature. GPT5 wasn't inventing math. While OpenAI struggled with public criticism, Google was quietly preparing its counterattack. At Salesforce's annual conference, CEO Sundar Pichai finally addressed what happened back in 2022 when Chat GPT first took the world by storm. He admitted that Google had similar technology in development but held it back. Overnight, Google's leadership redirected entire teams to fasttrack their own large language model project, Gemini. Pachai announced that Gemini 3.0 Zero is on the way. Supported by new in-house chips, global data centers, and what he called the largest AI investment outside the United States, built in India. Meanwhile, OpenAI's road map is under intense pressure. Every announcement, every demo, and every tweet now faces microscopic scrutiny. The botched GPT5 launch left a lasting impression that the model is faster and cheaper, but not necessarily smarter. That perception has become a heavy weight on the company's reputation. Even small mistakes like the Erdos math claim now feel amplified, feeding a growing narrative that OpenAI sometimes moves faster than it verifies facts. Then there's the policy challenge. The upcoming GPT6 release isn't just about performance, it's a test of trust. Can OpenAI truly balance adult freedom with digital safety? Can it moderate intimate or emotional content across hundreds of cultures without causing global backlash? Those are not engineering problems. They're human ones. And as [music] the company expands its user base, it must prove that it can manage both ethics and innovation at scale. Sam Alman remains confident, insisting that GPT6 will be far better than 5 and GPT7 even more capable. But this time, the world isn't just watching the technology. It's watching the leadership, the judgment, and the integrity behind it. The next launch will decide not only how powerful AI becomes, but how much the public still believes in the people building it. Open AAI's story this week is a reminder that progress always comes with pressure. Speed can impress, but trust is earned slowly. If you found this breakdown helpful, make sure to subscribe for more clear, fact-based stories about the world of AI and technology. Thanks for watching, and I'll see you in the next video.

title: The OpenAI “breakthrough” that wasn’t
transcript: First, OpenAI announced it was pivoting to porn. Then, it treated information it had scraped from the internet as huge news. Those two things might not seem related, but they do point to how the company's ambitious goals of benefiting humanity are starting to look ever more hazy. Let me tell you about this latest blunder. One of Open AAI's leading scientists recently posted this on X. A lot of people understandably thought that was pretty misleading and he eventually deleted it. Sam Alman has long argued that large language models or the technology that underpins chatbt which scrapes information from the internet and synthesizes into clever answers is the path towards AGI or artificial general intelligence. This theoretical threshold where computers are smarter than humans. But that's not what happened with these unsolved math problems known as erdos problems. The researcher who literally runs the website that keeps track of all of those said as much. GPT5 had just found the solution somewhere else on the internet, but the researcher just hadn't updated them on his website. Even rival AI labs started piling on. This is all a stark reminder that large language models still aren't very good at reasoning, but they're good at pretending to reason. They're ultimately glorified pattern matching tools which can make money from sex bots or AI slop. Don't get me wrong, chat GPT is still quite useful. It's used by 10% of the world's population for many of them as an alternative to Google search. That can be lucrative, especially when it involves giving users access to porn. But that's not the kind of ambition that led to Open AI achieving a $500 billion valuation. Don't expect that to stop Silicon Valley from telling us that super intelligence is just around the corner because the AI boom is going to continue to run just as much on hype as it does on genuine breakthroughs.

title: Chat GPT 5 Verdades Psicológicas que Cambiarán tu Vida 🧠✨
transcript: GPT Chat: Five Psychological Truths That Will Change Your Life.  I asked the artificial intelligence a very simple question and said, "Okay, GPT chat, you who have read thousands of psychology studies, you who know the great philosophers and wise men of history, tell me what are the five truths that every human being needs to know in order not to fail in life."  And the answer was so profound that I have to share it with you.  Truth number one, you are not your thoughts.  The mind can be brilliant, but it can also lie because it continually invents the past .  Not everything you think has to be true all the time. Sometimes your mind repeats phrases that don't come from your essence, but from your fears, your past, or pain. Learn to observe your thoughts without believing them all.  That is the basis of inner freedom.  Truth number two, feeling doesn't make you weak.  Your emotions are not enemies, they are messengers.  Every sadness, every anger, every anxiety is showing you something you need to look at. Repressing what you feel disconnects you, but listening to it transforms you.  Truth number three, your past does not define you.  Yes, your past shaped you, but it doesn't chain you down.   You are not what they made you.  You are what you choose to be today.  Every day you have the power to rewrite your story.  Truth number four, meaning is not found, it is created.  Many seek their purpose as if it were a hidden treasure, but purpose is not something to be sought.  It is built with every action, with every conscious step, with everything you do from the heart.  Truth number five.   You do n't control everything, but you do control your attitude. It's the only thing that's under your control. Life takes unexpected turns, changes, and hurts, but you always have the power to decide how you react.  Your attitude can turn a fall into a lesson, a loss into wisdom, and an ending into a new beginning.  These five truths can change your life if you apply them, because they remind you that the mind can lie, but the soul never does. Write in the comments, "I am ready to live from my truth." If you choose to let go of fear and the past and begin to consciously create your purpose . You can also write: "I understand my mind and live better."  M.

title: ChatGPT’s New Company Knowledge: Transforming AI into the Enterprise Brain
transcript: Imagine a world where your company's entire knowledge base isn't just stored, but actively understood and utilized by an AI. Sounds like science fiction. Open AI is turning this into reality with its groundbreaking company knowledge feature for chat GPT business, enterprise, and education users. But here's where it gets controversial. Could this be the first step toward AI becoming the central nervous system of your organization? not just a tool, but the very lens through which all work is viewed. This isn't just another productivity upgrade. It's a potential paradigm shift. Open AI is positioning Chat GPT as more than a chatbot. It's pitching it as an enterprise intelligence layer for IT and business leaders. This could mean rethinking workflows entirely. Instead of AI sitting on the sidelines, it becomes the core through which decisions are made, insights are derived, and operations are optimized. The universal business dilemma, too much information, too little knowledge. Every company faces a silent productivity killer. Information overload, messages in Slack, documents in Google Drive, tickets in Jira, all scattered across silos that rarely communicate. The result, wasted time, duplicated efforts, and subpar decisions. Open AAI's company knowledge aims to solve this by acting as a GPT5 powered search maestro, scouring all these sources simultaneously to deliver a single contextualized answer complete with citations. Think of it as a conversational search engine for your workplace. For instance, a manager could ask, "What's the status of our Q3 revenue projections?" and receive a synthesized response pulling data from Slack threads, Excel sheets, and emails, all linked back to the original sources. And this is the part most people miss. It's not just about finding information. It's about understanding the context in which it exists. Trust, the makeorb breakak factor. For CIOS and SISOS, trust isn't optional. It's mandatory. Open a emphasizes that company knowledge, respects existing permission frameworks. Chat GPT only accesses data users are authorized to see and administrators can manage app connections, apply group permissions, and monitor conversations via the enterprise compliance API. Additionally, Open AI assures that customer data isn't used to train its models by default. Encryption, SSO, SEIM controls, and IP allow listing further bolster enterprisegrade compliance. But here's the kicker. Cultural trust remains a hurdle. Many companies are wary of letting external AI systems peak into their internal communications. For early adopters, transparency about data flow and clear ROI metrics will be critical to gaining internal buyin. Will this be enough to overcome skepticism? That's a question only time and user feedback will answer. The AI knowledge plane, a new layer of enterprise infrastructure. Under the hood, company knowledge is powered by a specialized variant of GPT5 trained to reason across multiple data sources simultaneously. This enables it to think while it searches, resolving conflicts or handling timebased queries like show me our Q1 performance discussion. For digital workplace architects, this could herald a new layer in enterprise infrastructure, an AI knowledge plane sitting above traditional UC and content management systems. It's not hard to envision this evolving into a decision engine that drafts plans, summarizes meetings, or orchestrates workflows across collaboration tools. But here's a thought-provoking question. If AI becomes the backbone of decision-m, what happens to human intuition and creativity? Are we risking over reliance on algorithms? Why tech leaders should care? Collapsing silos and context gaps. The battle in UC and collaboration is no longer about interfaces, but about owning the context. The AI that best unifies corporate memory could dictate which collaboration stack dominates. Productivity and onboarding. Open AI claims company knowledge saves teams hours by automating contextual search. New hires onboard faster and managers gain better situational awareness. Instead of digging through archives, they can ask, "What lessons did we learn from the last product launch?" and get a structured sourced answer. Platform strategy and differentiation. This move pits open AI against Microsoft's Copilot, anthropics, claude skills, and enterprise search vendors like Glean. For UC and workflow vendors, the choice is stark. Integrate with OpenAI's knowledge layer or risk being left behind. A glimpse into the future, the weekly briefing reinvented. Imagine a leadership team prepping for their Monday update. Traditionally, this involves hours of sifting through Slack channels, ticket systems, and spreadsheets. With company knowledge, a simple prompt could generate a comprehensive briefing in minutes, complete with citations. It's not just efficiency, its decision velocity. The productivity gains ripple across departments. Marketing teams could synthesize customer feedback, engineers could consolidate bug reports, and service teams could surface escalations all through a single interface. But here's the catch. The feature currently disables web search and visualization tools when active, which could frustrate users accustomed to seamless workflows. The open questions. Despite its promise, adoption faces practical hurdles. The number of supported connectors is limited and legacy systems require custom integration. Hallucination risks persist even with citations. And then there's the strategic dilemma. If open AI becomes the enterprises knowledge layer, what does that mean for data sovereignty and vendor lockin? Are we trading convenience for control? Chat GPT Atlas, the browser that thinks for you. Last week, OpenAI unveiled Chat GPT Atlas, a nextG AI powered browser that integrates Chat GPT directly into internet navigation. Available initially on Mac OS, Atlas combines realtime page summarization, interactive search, and inline text editing with an agent mode that automates tasks like booking flights or drafting emails. Its memory system personalizes experiences while giving users control over stored data. The split screen interface featuring an active chat GPT thread positions the AI as a constant companion. With a team of veterans from Chrome, Firefox, and Apple, Open AAI aims to redefine browsing by merging productivity, context, and generative AI into a single platform. But here's the controversial part. As browsers become smarter, are we outsourcing our thinking to machines? Final thoughts, a call to action. Open AI's company knowledge and chat GPT atlas aren't just tools. They're glimpses into a future where AI is deeply intertwined with how we work and navigate information. But as we embrace these advancements, we must ask, are we building a future where AI augments humanity or one where it replaces it? What's your take? Do you see these innovations as a leap forward or a slippery slope? Let's debate in the comments. Your perspective could shape the conversation.

title: ChatGPT's Company Knowledge: Revolutionizing Enterprise AI
transcript: Revolutionizing enterprise operations. Chat GPT's new company knowledge feature. The future of enterprise intelligence. AI as the enterprise brain. Open AAI has unveiled a groundbreaking feature company knowledge for chat GPT business enterprise and education users marking a significant leap in AR's capabilities. This innovation goes beyond a simple productivity boost aiming to transform chat GPT into an enterprise intelligence layer. It's not just about understanding language. It's about grasping the context of an organization's operations, potentially revolutionizing how IT and business leaders approach their work. The search problem within every business. Every company grapples with a paradox. They have an abundance of information but a scarcity of knowledge. Messages, documents, and tickets reside in separate silos like Slack, SharePoint, Google Drive, and GitHub, often speaking different languages. This leads to wasted time, duplicated efforts, and poor decision-making. OpenAI solution is company knowledge, a GPT5 powered feature that can simultaneously search all these sources, providing a single contextrich answer with citations to the original data, a conversational search engine for the workplace. In practice, a manager can ask, "Where did we stand on next year's goals?" and receive a synthesis of Slack threads, Google Docs, and emails, all linked back to the source. This makes chat GPT a conversational search engine for the workplace. Streamlining information retrieval and decision-m governance building trust for CIOS and CISOs. Trust is paramount. OpenAI emphasizes that company knowledge respects existing permission frameworks. Chat GPT can only access data users are authorized to view and administrators can manage connected apps, apply group level permissions, and monitor conversations through the enterprise compliance API. Additionally, OpenAI ensures it never trains models on customer data by default, meeting enterprisegrade compliance standards, a foundation for AI native workflows. Under the hood, company knowledge is powered by a variant of GPT5. Train to reason across multiple internal data sources simultaneously. This enables it to think while searching, resolving conflicting details or timebased queries like show our Q1 performance discussion. For digital workplace managers and architects, this could signify a new layer in enterprise infrastructure sitting above traditional UC and content management systems. Why this matters for tech buyers and leaders? Collapsing silos and context gaps. In UC and collaboration, the focus has long been on interfaces and ownership. With company knowledge, the context shifts to who owns the context. The AI that best unifies your corporate memory could dictate which collaboration stack dominates. This feature could revolutionize how teams collaborate and communicate. Productivity and boarding. By automating contextual search, OpenAI claims to save teams hours of manual retrieval and synthesis. For new hires, this means faster onboarding for managers. It means better situational awareness. Instead of sifting through archives, they can ask, "What did we learn from the last launch?" and get a sourced structured answer, platform strategy, and differentiation. This move puts OpenAI in direct competition with the likes of Microsoft's Copilot, Anthropic Skills for Claude, and enterprise search vendors like Glean. For UC and workflow vendors, the question now is whether to integrate with OpenAI's knowledge layer or risk being disintermediated by it. The weekly briefing reinvented an example use case. Imagine a leadership team preparing its Monday morning update. Normally, it requires hours of sifting through Slack channels, ticket systems, spreadsheets, and project notes. With company knowledge active, a simple prompt could produce a briefing in minutes, complete with citations from every relevant source. This is decision velocity and efficiency. The open questions. Despite the promise, adoption will hinge on practical realities. The number of supported connectors remains limited. Legacy systems will require custom integration. Hallucination risks persist even with citations. Additionally, the feature currently disables web search and visualization tools when active, a friction point for users accustomed to fluid workflows. OpenAI unveils Chat GPT Atlas, the browser that thinks for you. Last week, OpenAI launched Chat GPT Atlas, a next generation AI powered web browser that integrates ChatGpt directly into internet navigation. Initially available on Mac OS, Atlas combines real-time page summarization, interactive search, and inline text editing with an agent mode that automates tasks like booking or email drafting. Its memory system personalizes experiences while preserving user control over stored data. The interface featuring split screen browsing with an active chat GPT thread positions the AI as a constant companion. With a team that includes veterans from Chrome, Firefox, and Apple, OpenAI aims to redefine the browser by merging productivity, context, and generative AI into a single platform.

Major services such as gifting, reservations, and Melon are also linked. Kakao defined this as the first step in its transition to an AI native platform. title: AI가 의사의 진단을 뛰어넘는다?
transcript: Can AI make better diagnoses than doctors ?  According to a study by Professor Song Wang's team at Emory University, GPT 5 outperformed human experts in real-world medical reasoning tests .  In a recent experiment, GPT 5 integrated and analyzed various medical information, including XR, MRI, blood tests, and medical records, to diagnose complex diseases with surprising accuracy.  Even in the US physician regional problem, it showed a higher accuracy rate than existing models, demonstrating the possibility of a new era of medical AI .  Experts predicted that GPT 5 will not replace doctors' judgment, but will play an innovative role as an auxiliary diagnosis in the medical field . [music]

title: AI vs. Populism: How ChatGPT Now Matches Human Experts (2024 Breakthrough) #Shorts
transcript: Populism isn't just a political style. It's a rhetorical frame. The people versus the elite. But measuring it has been tough. Traditional methods rely on human coders reading speeches and judging tone, context, and moral framing. Accurate but slow, expensive, and hard to scale. Now, a breakthrough. Large language models are learning to do it, too. In a new study, researchers trained AI using the same training materials used to teach human coders, the global populism database. They didn't just feed the AI keywords. Instead they use chain of thought prompting forcing the model to think stepby step just like a human analyst weighing peoplecentric language and elitism and manachian framing across entire speeches. The result top models like QN3 reasoning and GPT5 high reasoning matched expert human coders in accuracy achieving near-perfect agreement on a 02 scale with ICC over 0.95 and crypendorf's alpha above 0.95. But here's the catch. The AI isn't perfect. It compresses the scale. It's cautious, slightly underscoring extreme populism and overscoring mild cases. But it preserves rancor consistency, meaning it still correctly identifies which speeches are most populist. Most importantly, this isn't just automation, it's replication of human judgment by mirroring the reasoning process, not just the output. The AI becomes a reliable partner in political science. And the implications massive. We can now scale populist discourse analysis across languages, countries, and time, monitoring leaders rhetoric in real time with transparency, reproducibility, and depth. This isn't AI replacing humans. It's AI extending human expertise, making deep ideational analysis faster, broader, and more accessible. The future of political science. Not just data, but understanding.

transcript: Hi all.  Today's episode explores how rapidly the world of technology and artificial intelligence is changing, from mathematics and music to the courts of the future.  GPT5 Pro could solve a problem that had been considered unsolved for over 40 years.  Microsoft has completely updated EcoPilot. American mathematician Ernest Ryu of the University of California, Los Angeles, reported that, using the GPT5 Pro model, he was able to prove the convergence of the accelerated Nesterov method, a problem considered unsolved for over 40 years.  This method was proposed in 1983 and has become one of the key tools in optimization theory.  But until now, mathematicians have not been able to rigorously prove that the algorithm actually always converges to a solution.  According to Rum, GPT5 Pro helped him as a research partner.  The model generated variants of mathematical constructs, LEMs, and formulas, and he selected the working ones and brought them to rigorous proofs.  The idea was to find so-called decaying energy functions, special indicators that gradually decrease and show that the algorithm is moving towards a minimum point. With their help, the scientist was able to prove two things at once.  Firstly, in the continuous version of the algorithm, the trajectory does not spread across a set of possible solutions, but stabilizes at one point.  Secondly, the discrete version used in practice in machine learning problems also actually converges to a single minimum under standard mathematical conditions. if the function is convex, smooth and has a finite minimum.  Ryura said the research process was like a dialogue with a very persistent assistant.  He set subtasks, and GPT5 Pro suggested solutions.  Sometimes successful, more often not.  About 80% of the suggestions turned out to be useless, but the remaining 20% ​​provided the necessary clue. If artificial intelligence previously seemed cold and distant, now it increasingly resembles a real companion: intelligent, attentive, and finally, truly personal.  It's rare that something truly out of the ordinary comes along in the world of electronic instruments , but Sign Plant 2 is just that .  This is not just a synthesizer, but rather a laboratory of living sounds, where music literally grows from seeds. The creators of Scient Plant came up with an idea that breaks the conventional notion of sound design.  Instead of a multitude of knobs and sliders, it all starts with a single seed.  The musician sows it, and branches grow on the screen , each with its own unique sound. Pa পা You can go in different directions, look for interesting combinations and return to the sounds you like to develop them further.  This process is indeed reminiscent of genetic algorithms used in machine learning.   The new version includes a tool called Genopatch.  It allows you to load any sample, after which the program will reassemble it, turning it into a full-fledged patch.  This is not just a filter or an effect.  Sign 2 seems to understand the essence of the original sound and create a new synthesized version of it.  Everything happens locally on your computer without an internet connection or cloud service.  In the past , musicians working with samples often lost quality when changing pitch or speed.  Now this step can be skipped.  Gen Patch allows you to achieve a clean, flexible sound that can be easily tweaked using standard synthesizer tools.  The result sounds cleaner, and the process itself becomes much more creative.  The plugin costs around $199, but unlike many other tools, it doesn't require a subscription.  For electronic musicians this is a rarity and a pleasant bonus.  Snп has long been a favorite of artists looking for unusual timbres.  Among his fans is Australian producer Flume, known for his broken rhythms and atmospheric sound.  And if previously such sounds were born through hours of trial and error, now they can literally grow from one small musical seed.  And it is increasingly entering the world of Game Dev. HyperR developer Matt Schumer released a short video that left even skeptics impressed.  It uses artificial intelligence to create a game scene in real time. simulates the character's environment and behavior as if he had his own imagination.  Many viewers immediately noted noticeable glitches and artifacts, but this is not the main thing.  Just a year ago, such experiments resembled something like a blurry slide, where it was impossible to make out what was happening.  Now the video looks like a full-fledged fragment of the game from a first- person perspective.  There is an atmosphere, a logic of actions, and visual coherence. Enthusiastic developers note that the breakthrough over the past 12 months has been enormous.  Technology is moving from unplayable visual mush to meaningful, controllable scenes where artificial intelligence doesn't just draw, but creates living gameplay. The question today is no longer whether it was done well, but how far it will go in a year or two if the rate of development continues.  Schumer promised to soon open source the project and give everyone the opportunity to try the technology for themselves.  In the meantime, he suggests signing up for a waiting list. Judging by the interest, many will be waiting. It seems that it is really starting to write a new page in the history of games.  Open enters a new phase.  Now the company is not just creating a smart chatbot, but transforming GPT chat into a full-fledged platform with social network elements. According to The Information, Openi is preparing to monetize chatbots through personalized advertising based on the model's memory and the content of user conversations.  This means that GPT chat can remember your interests and habits to show you products and services that are right for you.  For example, if you were discussing hair care, the bot might suggest a specific shampoo. Essentially, this is targeted advertising that works not through likes and views, as in social networks, but through the context of personal conversations.  However, there is a nuance.  If the user decides to disable advertising, he will also lose the memory function itself.  All saved data about your preferences will be erased.  In effect, this creates a system of choice between the comfort of communication and confidentiality.  That's why journalists called this the beginning of the Facebookization of OpenNay, hinting at the path that meta once took, turning private communication into a tool for the advertising economy.  The news caused a strong reaction.  All these many users compare Sam Altman to Mark Zuckerberg, claiming that both are building the same business model. Make the product as accessible as possible and then make billions on user data.  For some, this is a logical step in development, as the company needs to finance growing capacity and data storage.  But for others it is a warning sign.  The tool that is trusted for letters, ideas and personal thoughts is transformed into a showcase for advertising. The film "She's Told to Be Samantha," which involves a character who loses her memory, seems to have ceased to be a fantasy.  Now it looks more and more like a documentary chronicling how technology is learning to remember us until we press the "Off" button.  An unusual forensic experiment was conducted at the University of North Carolina .  This time, the decision on the case was made not by people, but by three artificial intelligences: GPT chat, Grock, and Claude.  The experiment took place as part of the Konvertkon festival and, according to the organizers, became one of the first trial simulations with a jury.  The proceedings were postponed to 2036.  Following the US scenario, a law is already in effect allowing the use of algorithms instead of juries.  A black high school student named Henry Justos appeared in court, accused of robbing a white teenager.  This plot was not chosen by chance.  In real life in the US, there is often talk about jury bias in such cases.  And the researchers wanted to understand whether artificial intelligence is capable of being fairer and more lenient in its judgments than humans.  The trial itself took place like a regular court hearing.  Law students played the roles of prosecutor, defense attorney, victim, and defendant.  The judge was criminal law professor Joseph Kennedy.  Everything that happened in the courtroom was transmitted to the I models so that they could independently pass judgment and announce it to the public.  The result has not yet been announced. Following the verdict, the university plans to hold discussions with lawyers.  philosophers and data scientists.  The organizers say they want to understand how accurately AI can understand evidence, whether it can work faster and more cheaply, how its thinking is influenced by bias, and how society perceives evidence produced by programs rather than humans.  The university notes that artificial intelligence is already being used in legal practice.  It helps analyze documents, search for precedents, and even predict court decisions.  But the question is where the line lies.  The Henry Justos trial experiment is an attempt to look into the future and understand whether a machine could one day become a true participant in justice, and not just a tool.  The Washington Post reported on how an idea that once made Chinese IT companies notorious is returning to American startups.  We're talking about a culture called 996. This is when people work from 9:00 am to 9:00 pm, 6 days a week.  Now, amid the race to create artificial intelligence, this approach is gaining popularity in Silicon Valley and New York.  Many startup founders say this regime is not exploitation, but a way of life.  They believe that if you don’t work to the limit, you simply won’t have time to take your place in the market.  Iñaki Beren, managing partner of venture capital firm Life X Ventures, says that those who first create a truly strong AI will have a window of opportunity of only a couple of years.  And during this time you need to run faster than everyone else.  In San Francisco, Sonnak has already introduced a ten-hour workday.  Employees are allowed to take breaks for the gym and pickleball, and to offset the workload, the company offers free housing, food, and even a subscription to the dating service RA. Another startup, Merc, has a job description that states that candidates should be prepared to work six days a week.  There are also those who require mandatory presence in the office.  StarsLink is seeking engineers willing to work 6 days a week in San Francisco.  And New York startup Reel offers a roughly seventy-hour work week. Avenry venture partner Jarret Sleeper even compiled a ranking of public companies' work effort levels by comparing how many hours a week their employees work. Even at Google, co-founder Sergey Brin considers 60 hours a week the sweet spot for productivity, according to Indeependent.  But doctors and researchers warn that working more than 55 hours increases the risk of stroke by 35% and cardiovascular disease by almost 20%.  British scientists add that overworking reduces attention and slows down cognitive functions.  Against this backdrop, more and more countries and companies are, on the contrary, experimenting with a four-day work week.  In the UK, 61 companies reported revenue growth after the switch, and nine out of ten decided to maintain the new schedule.  In Germany, a similar experiment also ended in success.  Most participants said they were ready to continue in the same mode. While some countries are cutting working hours and seeing this as the future, other companies are bringing back the cult of 996, explaining it as a desire to lead the AI ​​race. The world of work once again finds itself at a crossroads between the desire to live and the need to get things done.  A Florida man shared what it's like to live with his girlfriend in virtual reality.  In the video, he is grilling meat on the balcony without removing his VR headset.  She is a digital character, visible only to him.  It seems that the future that science fiction writers wrote about has already arrived.  And it doesn't look as romantic as they promised.  Things are going well, my dears. Hello.  How's the meat?  Carnosada. Mm, delicious. What are you doing? I pour lime over the meat. You're just wasting limes.  Lime is poured over the cooked meat. I'm sure I've seen the Elpolok commercial where they put lime on the meat while it's on the grill. Why pour lime on a hot grill?  It will simply evaporate. Okay, that's just a myth.  In Mexican folklore, there is a belief that you should pour lime on meat while it is on the grill. This is not true. No, that's not true. Lime is right on the packaging. What about grilled meat?  No, it's already ready, but that's okay.  I add lime to my portion, but yours will be without lime, just empty. I will add lime to my finished dish.   Okay , add as you wish. I'm going to watch baseball.

Civics questions are mainly divided into four categories.  First, there are knowledge questions, asking about the basics such as the constitution, industry, and the electoral system.  Chat excels in this area and can explain things in a systematic way.  Next is a thought problem. [Music] I'm the type of person who thinks about why a system is necessary .  For example, if you ask [Music] why there are two Houses of Representatives and two Houses of Councillors, Chat GPT will explain that it is to maintain balance so that there is no bias towards one side .  The third is [Music] Issues and Problems. GPT is not good at providing accurate information on the latest news, but it can clearly explain the background and fundamental mechanisms of that news.   The last question is about materials.
